text: The video shows the optical entry into the retromuscular space, providing a view of the anatomical structures and surgical field as the procedure begins.
The surgeon initiates insufflation through the applied medical first entry trocar, revealing the rectus muscle in the surgical field.
The surgeon accesses the retro rectus areolar plane and performs dissection using a cannula, followed by the placement of ports to initiate the robotic operation.
The surgeon performs a crossover maneuver within the retromuscular space, as part of the ETEP procedure.
The surgeon applies traction on the tissue to develop the preperitoneal dissection, targeting the contralateral posterior sheath.
The surgeon develops the space in the abdominal area, revealing sutures from a previous epigastric hernia repair located in the middle of the diastasis.
The surgical field shows an umbilical hernia located lower down, yet to be encountered, with a standard crossover technique being applied.
The surgeon attempts to create a broad space but miscalibrates the retro-rectus space, inadvertently extending the dissection medially.
The surgeon makes an incision through the linea alba, revealing the anatomical structure beneath.
The surgeon develops the contralateral retro-rectus space and proceeds with the reduction of an umbilical hernia, addressing chronically incarcerated preperitoneal fat.

The surgeon performs a reduction of the herniated preperitoneal fat, followed by limited preperitoneal dissection to separate the peritoneum from the posterior sheath, allowing for a safe incision without affecting underlying contents.
The surgeon has addressed the diastasis and is now expanding the retrorectus space to facilitate broad mesh coverage along the midline.
The video shows the patient's abdominal wall with well-developed musculature and a broad rectus width, measuring approximately ten to eleven centimeters on each side.
The surgeon performs anterior fascial closure, starting below the hernia and diastasis, placating and reinforcing the fascia.
The surgeon uses a number one permanent V-lock suture to perform closure or reinforcement in the surgical area.
The surgeon navigates around the area of the umbilical hernia, using an eighteen-inch suture to address the hernia site.
The surgeon performs the first round of suture tightening to remove redundancy without drawing tissues closer together, preparing to use multiple sutures to placate the diastasis.

The surgeon performs sequential tightening during placation, running sutures back over themselves to draw the tissue together. The sutures are placed between previous bites to avoid creating too many holes in the fascia.
The surgeon completes the placation and prepares for mesh placement, focusing on subtle nuances in technique.
The surgeon places a 24 by 30 centimeter mesh over the surgical area, accommodating the patient's anatomy.
